nectarivores.name: Purple sunbird
nectarivores.desc: Most commonly seen sunbird in Bengaluru parks and gardens. Males display spectacular iridescent purple-black plumage in breeding season. Strongly territorial — one pair will defend a flowering garden aggressively.
tips: Plant tubular Hibiscus as a hedge along a fence — one pair will nest within it by Year 2
Include a Jacaranda — Purple sunbirds show a documented preference for Jacaranda blossoms in Bengaluru specifically
Avoid pesticides on flowering shrubs — sunbirds also glean small insects from flower structures for protein

nectarivores.name: Purple-rumped sunbird
nectarivores.desc: Most urban-tolerant sunbird — completely comfortable around human habitation. Males have iridescent blue-green crown, maroon back and distinctive violet rump patch. One of the smallest birds in South India.
tips: This species will nest in garden shrubs within 0.5 m of human activity
Muntingia calabura (Singapore Cherry) is the single most reliable attractant documented in Bengaluru gardens
Place Ixora clusters at 1–2 m height — they prefer perch-feeding over hovering

nectarivores.name: Loten's sunbird
nectarivores.desc: Distinguished by its noticeably longer, more decurved bill — an adaptation for deep tubular flowers. The long bill gives it access to nectar sources other sunbirds cannot reach.
tips: Specifically plant deep-tubular flowers — its long bill is an advantage here and reduces competition with other sunbirds
Indian Coral Tree (Erythrina indica) is particularly documented as a magnet for Loten's sunbird in South India

nectarivores.name: Crimson-backed sunbird
nectarivores.desc: Endemic to the Western Ghats — the smallest sunbird in India. Appears at Nandi Hills and farm landscapes near the Ghats edge during monsoon. A strong ecological indicator of Western Ghats habitat connectivity.
tips: Presence of this species indicates Western Ghats ecological connectivity — a premium biodiversity indicator
Document any sighting immediately — this goes into your Biodiversity Impact Report as a headline indicator

nectarivores.name: Golden-fronted leafbird
nectarivores.desc: Stunning emerald-green bird with a golden forehead and blue throat patch. Often misidentified as a parrot. Feeds on nectar, fruits and insects — a true ecological generalist. Found in wooded gardens with mature canopy trees.
tips: Requires mature canopy trees — will not colonise young gardens
Its rich call is a strong landscape health indicator — if you hear it, your habitat layering is working

nectarivores.name: Jerdon's leafbird
nectarivores.desc: Similar to the Golden-fronted leafbird but slightly smaller. Found in wooded gardens and forest-edge habitats. Both leafbird species together indicate a structurally complex, mature landscape.
tips: Both leafbirds together confirm multi-layer habitat complexity
Dense canopy cover is the primary habitat requirement — plan for at minimum 35% canopy cover

nectarivores.name: Pale-billed flowerpecker
nectarivores.desc: One of the smallest birds in South India. An important seed disperser for mistletoe plants which then grow on canopy trees. Year-round resident throughout Bengaluru.
tips: Singapore Cherry (Muntingia calabura) is the single most reliable tree for attracting flowerpeckers in Bengaluru
Plant at least 2–3 Singapore Cherry trees — fast growing, flowers and fruits year-round

nectarivores.name: Thick-billed flowerpecker
nectarivores.desc: Rare in Bengaluru — recorded occasionally at sites with dense fruiting canopy. A bonus species — if recorded, confirms exceptional fruit tree diversity. Appearance timeline is typically Year 5–7 after planting.
tips: Plant Ficus species at Year 1 — this species will arrive when the fig trees mature in Year 5–7
Its presence confirms exceptional ecological maturation of your landscape

nectarivores.name: Tickell's flowerpecker
nectarivores.desc: Resident breeder across Bengaluru. Extremely active and fast-moving. Commonly observed at Singapore Cherry trees and fruiting garden shrubs. Nests in tiny purse-shaped structures hanging from branch tips.
tips: Almost identical behaviour to Pale-billed flowerpecker — both will colonise the same garden
Very fast-moving — easier to detect by call (sharp, rapid chip-chip) than by sight
